author: @Alacritous
text: I have a bunch of momentary switches with a built-in LED which I bought for this kind of use case. Software switching something on and off. If you use an ON-OFF switch to turn something on and off, it's stuck in that position and can confuse things if you want to also control it with a microcontroller or computer. With a momentary switch with an LED in it, I push the switch to turn something on or off and the microcontroller controls the state of the LED and the state of the device being controlled. The LED on the switch can show the state of the device no matter where it is switched from.

author: @bretthorwood9396
text: If you like Arduino you will probably get interested in things that go beyond that. You will need extra external boards like PWM boards for motors etc that are too big to be a shield. I hope this makes sense. So download Kicad from the CERN server. It will enable you to draw up schematics which you may like to design yourself of copy and modify from online resources eg product application notes from companies such  Texas Instruments or Vishay etc.Kicad will use the schematics as a reference eg linked to building the PWB ..PCB board. As it reduces mistakes you could make when you draw the board up by placing tracks between components.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: nice. You noticed the debounce counter in the code ? As I explained to you earlier .... switches bounce when switched on or off. This code only assumes the switch is on or off when it is stable for some time. If not there is no way telling what the output will be after you pressed the button.

author: @olivierroy1301
text: I want to make a resistive spot welder, but I will try to use a 555 timer. I seen a lot of schematics, but I've yet to try and make circuit with it. If I can be done with a few projects at work I may have time to try.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: "Taking it and using it" as Rob is doing is also among the best ways to learn how to write code yourself.  The more you use it, the more familiar it becomes and you begin to understand the language syntax.  Even in the 80's and 90's, I was entirely self-taught in programming by taking code I found in books, periodicals, newsletters, etc and plugging it in to my projects until I eventually knew how to write code myself.  These days, internet resources makes it soooo much easier!!  And I can't stress enough how helpful those "starter kits" are for the Arduino and Raspberry Pi!! 😊  What I wouldn't give to be a teen again today with all those resources at my fingertips!!

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: considering the mindset of the opensource community around arduino communy too those guys would probably be super happy to know it was useful to someone, more than worrying about being "shouted" out or "thanked" LOL (other than thanking them lets them know you liked it and found it useful, though I can see someone learning from it and going further they would be even happier , how I feel about the work I'm putting in on these 3D printer upgrades to allow printing metal and semiconductor material and such, thus why when anyone asks how they can help I ask for them to send me info on how to reach them so I can send them a care package when the final (able to print it self and fit in a box to be shipped even if as a kit) is done so I can mail them out to as many people around the world along with the files to print copies with them, and spread them to make it impossible for some government or corporation to force this geni back into the bottle :)

author: @dolanbaker
text: About 100% of the code I have ever "written" is copy paste tweak, tweak, tweak, tweak, tweak, tweak, f&*k, tweak, tweak, tweak, F&^%^%$G!, tweak, tweak & tweak until it does what I want it to do.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Using other people's code and adapting it is how everybody programs.  Unlike art, logic pretty much has a best path for small things.  

Large things is a different story.  Libraries of code can sometimes hide trojans that can compromise your system.  You need to know what each piece of code is doing if the library isn't from a trusted source before using those if you want to keep your system secure.  

Little prices if code like this are no problem though

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
"Using other people's code and adapting it is how everybody programs"

That's a bit of an exaggeration.
Using other people's code is a good way to learn how to program but, if you do not progress passed that to actually write code from scratch; your not really a programmer.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @scotttovey I don't think Colleen intended that statement to mean exclusively how people program.  But as a generalization, every programmer is going to grab and adapt code rather than write it from scratch whenever that's an option.  To do otherwise would be highly inefficient and unnecessary, unless there's a particular reason you want to challenge yourself.

author: @larrystrayer8336

Seems very similar in the 80’s when trying to learn a word processor or spreadsheet. All my instructions were geared to learning to program.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
Thing about being taught via  use, rather than coding... Is it often ignores skills one might already have... And then one needs to be taught again when new version of software comes out... 

Last time I did a refresher office software course... They went through every single way of opening up an item from drop down menu. Brain pretty much fell asleep, and I'd considered packing course in. When it came to exam I was not happy because well I'd have gotten exactly same mark if I'd sat it without having the refresher material. There was nothing in it that was specific to software! 

Then when they had us doing databases.. It was "use the assistant."  I'd never used such before but was able to see it just  writing SQL code... And that's pretty much just set theory that a preschooler  might understand. Pick out all brown Cows in a field etc. 5min later I'd picked up enough SQL to do more than the assistant could do. Lecturer didn't know what I was on about till I showed him the info via the  F1 key for help function... Which wasn't getting taught.

author: @BiosWars
text: We should really thank Fairchild and all its engineers.

replies.author: @edkoetsier339
replies.text: We can thank Fairchild for lots of things, but not Arduino. And you dont need to thank Atmel either. It was two Italian guys who designed the Arduino, using a common microcontroller.

replies.author: @BiosWars
replies.text: @edkoetsier339 Really think about that, for without Fairchild and their multitude of engineers, no one would be designing these micro devices. For off the achievements of these giants, we can enjoy such interesting possibilities. And that includes this ability.

replies.author: @edkoetsier339
replies.text: @BiosWars Thats like thanking prehistoric handaxe producing humans for IBM..

replies.author: @BiosWars
replies.text: ​@edkoetsier339 not likely, if it wasn't for Fairchild all micro IC's would not exist. try micro electronic architecture without it. For that matter PC would not be as small as they are.

replies.author: @edkoetsier339
replies.text: @BiosWars You really have an inflated view of Fairchilds role in the development of the semiconductor. You think nobody else was working on it? Who was granted the first patent? Texas Instruments.

author: @mfr58
text: Thanks, that was encouraging as I have avoided playing with Arduinos as I've not been keen spending ages learning coding. This seems like a way in. Great!

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: There's a lot of existing code out there that is just plug-and-play, with nothing more than maybe tweaking a few parameters or adding an extra instruction to do specifically what you want.  If you haven't yet done anything with an Arduino, I highly recommend one of those starter kits for around $30 or so.  They contain a bunch of easy projects and often contain the Arduino as well.  They're very good at step-by-step at just making it work, then you can spend time afterward to understand HOW it did.  😊
